fields.description:
# Fail-loud tombstone registry for removed environment and config inputs

Add a runtime tombstone registry that turns a retired, still-set env var or TOML
key into a targeted migration error (old name + replacement + removed-in version +
nonzero exit) when it affects store location, write/sync gates, auth, security, or
lifecycle policy — while genuinely-unknown keys keep the project's chosen
forward-compatibility policy. (Readiness Finding 5 — verified VALID against
`src/rebar/_deprecations.py`, `src/rebar/config.py`, `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`,
`tests/unit/test_config_tracker_dir.py`.)

## Why

The deprecation registry (`_deprecations.py`) now holds **only permanent aliases**;
every scheduled-removable surface was deleted in the pre-1.0 passes and survives
only in a historical comment block, so a removed name has no runtime signal path.
Consequently removed **env vars are fully silent** — not in `_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES`,
so they fall through to defaults with no warning at all — and removed **TOML keys**
get only the generic `_config_schema.py` "unknown key … (typo?)" warning-and-ignore
(hard error only under `REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error`), which neither names the
replacement nor says the key was removed. This bites security/state-sensitive
surfaces: `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` silently redirects the store to the default
`.tickets-tracker` (proven by `tests/unit/test_config_tracker_dir.py:42-47`);
`REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` (→ `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`) silently reverts a
live-sync write gate; `verify.require_verdict_for_close` (→
`verify.require_completion_verification_for_close`) silently drops a close-gate
policy; the legacy `rebar_id_guard_mode` id write-guard key is "no longer honored."
An operator upgrading with a stale deployment manifest gets a process that reads a
different store or a weaker policy than their configuration names.

## What

Adopt the nginx obsolete-directive + OpenSSH deprecated-keyword pattern (retain the
retired name explicitly; either report it and name the replacement, or reject
configuration when ignoring it could change effective behaviour — OpenSSH counts
bad options and terminates config loading):

- Add a `RemovedInput` tombstone registry with explicitly-defined vocabularies
  (in one place in `_deprecations.py`): `_TOMBSTONE_KINDS = frozenset({"env", "cfg",
  "file"})` and `_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIORS = frozenset({"error", "warn"})`. These are
  **separate** from the existing alias-registry `_KINDS = frozenset({"env", "cfg",
  "cli", "lib", "mcp"})` (which has no `"file"` and serves a different purpose) —
  do not overload it. A `RemovedInput` has (kind, name, replacement, removed_in,
  behavior), applied at config discovery / CLI startup **before defaults are selected**.
- **Distinct, un-swallowable error type — `class RemovedInputError(BaseException)`.**
  A retired error-class input raises a dedicated `RemovedInputError` that subclasses
  **`BaseException`** (NOT `Exception`, NOT `ConfigError`). This is the load-bearing
  choice, not stylistic: `tracker_dir()` wraps `load_config()` in `try/except
  ConfigError` (silent fallback to `.tickets-tracker`), and the MCP boot ensure-sweep
  wraps `_config.tracker_dir()` in broad `except Exception` blocks
  (`mcp_server.py:302-312` and `:311`). A `ConfigError`- or `Exception`-derived
  tombstone would be swallowed at one of those sites — re-creating the exact
  silent-redirect the gate must prevent. A `BaseException` subclass is caught by none
  of them and propagates to a top-level exit. (BaseException over `SystemExit` so it is
  an explicit rebar error type, not conflated with interpreter shutdown.)
- **Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers.** Belt-and-suspenders: the
  `except Exception` blocks at `mcp_server.py:302-312`/`:311` are updated to
  `except RemovedInputError: raise` (before the broad catch) so the intent is explicit
  and survives any future change that widens them to `except BaseException`. Grep the
  config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare `except:` / `except BaseException` swallows it.
- **Top-level CLI handler (clean exit, not a traceback).** Because `RemovedInputError`
  is a `BaseException`, an *unhandled* one would print a raw Python traceback. In
  `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()` the handler must wrap the **entire body after the
  CLI-override parsing / help-check block through the return** — NOT just `_dispatch()`
  — because `main()` has ~15 early-return intercepts (reconcile, review, review-code,
  enrich, verify-commit-ticket, identity, config, …) that run *before* `_dispatch()`
  and any of which can touch config. So `try: <all intercepts + _dispatch()> except
  RemovedInputError as e: sys.stderr.write(str(e) + "\n"); return 1` — every command
  surfaces the targeted migration message (old name + replacement + removed-in) and a
  nonzero exit, not a traceback. This coexists with `config validate`/`doctor` (which
  catches it *for reporting* without aborting).
- **MCP-server exit semantics (not just CLI).** The persistent `rebar-mcp` server must
  **fail startup / the write request hard** on a retired error-class input rather than
  downgrade it to a per-request error — verified by a test driving the MCP write path
  with the retired input set (warm cache) and asserting the operation is refused (the
  `BaseException` propagation guarantees this).
- **Cache-safe placement (important).** The env-var tombstone check must fire even
  on a warm `_RESULT_CACHE` hit — today `env_overrides()` runs only on a cache miss
  in `_resolve()`, so a check placed there would be skipped in the long-running MCP
  server. Place the env-var tombstone check at the **top of `load_config()`, before
  the cache lookup**, so it fires unconditionally; and add the tombstoned env-var
  names to `_env_signature()` (the same pattern already used for `_LEGACY_ENV_ALIASES`)
  so a retired var appearing mid-process invalidates the cache. The **file-presence**
  tombstone (`.rebar/config.conf`) is likewise an **unconditional check at the top of
  `load_config()` before the cache-key computation** — `if (repo_root(root) / '.rebar'
  / 'config.conf').exists(): raise RemovedInputError(...)` — NOT added to
  `_config_probe_paths()` (which runs only on a cache **miss** via `_resolve()`, so it
  would never fire on a warm MCP cache hit). **TOML-key tombstones**
  (`verify.require_verdict_for_close`, `reconciler.*`) have the SAME warm-cache trap,
  but a pre-cache raw-TOML scan would need to re-run config *discovery*
  (`_discover_project_config`) before the cache — heavy and duplicative. Instead, fold
  the TOML tombstone check **into `_resolve()`** (alongside the normal parse, on the
  cache-miss path) and record a "tombstone-hit" marker in the **validation tuple** that
  the cache already stores; the warm-cache path re-stats those config files on every
  hit (`_file_token`), so re-raising on the recorded marker makes the TOML tombstone
  fire on warm hits too — reusing the existing cache-validation machinery rather than a
  second discovery pass. So all three kinds (env, file, TOML) fire on both cold and
  warm cache.
- Hard-error (nonzero exit, message naming the old name, replacement, removed-in
  release, and a concise migration example) for retired inputs affecting store/data
  location, write targets, sync/reconcile, auth, security controls, verification /
  lifecycle policy, mutation enablement, or external publication.
- Warn-only for operationally inert retired inputs.
- Add a `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command that inspects process env,
  project TOML, user TOML, and file-supplied config against the active aliases and
  tombstones.
- **Prevent future silent removals (anti-regression guard).** The registry is only as
  complete as today's audit; nothing stops a *future* removal from being silent again.
  Add a CI check that compares the set of recognised config keys / env vars against a
  committed golden list and **fails if a key is removed without a corresponding
  tombstone entry** — so the tombstone registry stays complete by construction.
- Do not change the forward-compat policy for genuinely-unknown keys (they keep the
  WARN / `REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error` behaviour). The registry covers only
  known retired inputs that would otherwise be silently ignored or defaulted.

Seed the registry with the enumerated removed inputs (from the Finding 5 audit),
each individually named and classified:

- **env, error** (store/security/policy/sync): `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`→`REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`
  (store location); `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`→`REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`
  (live-sync write gate).
- **env, warn** (operationally inert): `REBAR_PUSH`→`REBAR_SYNC_PUSH` (push timing);
  `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES` and `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_RETRY_BUDGET` (both
  removed, no replacement — the ref lock self-heals).
- **cfg, error**: `verify.require_verdict_for_close`→
  `verify.require_completion_verification_for_close` (close-gate policy).
- **cfg, warn**: `reconciler.lock_backend` (ref lock is the only backend),
  `reconciler.lock_max_retries` (removed, ref lock self-heals).
- **file, error** (security): the legacy flat `.rebar/config.conf` file itself. Its
  reader was removed in DE7, so keys like `rebar_id_guard_mode` (the id write-guard —
  a security surface, replaced by env `REBAR_UNSAFE_ID_GUARD_BYPASS`) are silently
  inert if left in a stale file. Handle this as a **file-presence** tombstone at
  discovery (detect a leftover `.rebar/config.conf` and error, naming `rebar.toml` /
  `[tool.rebar]` as the replacement and the env var for the guard) — **not** as a
  `_config_schema.py` TOML-key tombstone, since the removed key never reaches the
  TOML parser (a TOML-routed tombstone for it would be permanently-unreachable dead
  code).
- **`REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`→`REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS`** lives in the optional `rebar.llm`
  layer (not `config.py`/`_config_schema.py`), so its tombstone is applied in the LLM
  config load path (see Scope), classified **warn** (LLM step budget).

### Implementation sequencing (thin vertical slice first)

Deliver as staged, independently-landable changes so the riskiest assumption is proven
before the rest is built on it:

1. **Slice 1 — de-risk the propagation (land first).** The `RemovedInput` registry +
   `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` + the cache-safe env check at the top of
   `load_config()` + the rewritten `tracker_dir` test + the MCP re-raise. This proves
   `BaseException` propagates through the known MCP handlers (`mcp_server.py:302-312`/
   `:311`) without being swallowed — the single riskiest piece.
2. **Slice 2 — breadth on top.** The file-presence (`.rebar/config.conf`) tombstone,
   the `rebar.llm` `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone, the `config validate`/`doctor`
   command, the CI golden-file anti-regression guard, and docs.

Each slice is a stacked change closed only after its own CI is green.

## Scope

- `src/rebar/_deprecations.py` — add the tombstone registry alongside the alias
  registry, defining `_TOMBSTONE_KINDS = frozenset({"env","cfg","file"})` and
  `_TOMBSTONE_BEHAVIORS = frozenset({"error","warn"})` (separate from the existing
  `_KINDS`); update `test_deprecation_registry` for the new vocabularies.
- `src/rebar/config.py` — apply the env-var, file-presence, AND tombstoned-TOML-key
  checks at the **top of `load_config()` (before the `_RESULT_CACHE` lookup)** so they
  fire even on a warm cache hit; add tombstoned env-var names to `_env_signature()` and
  the tombstoned TOML key paths to the cache key.
- `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()` — wrap the whole body (all ~15 early-return
  intercepts + `_dispatch()`) in `except RemovedInputError as e: sys.stderr.write(...);
  return 1` (clean exit for every command, no traceback).
- `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — route a known retired **TOML** key
  (`verify.require_verdict_for_close`, `reconciler.*`) to a targeted removed-key error
  rather than the generic "typo?" warning.
- `src/rebar/llm/` (`LLMConfig.from_env()`) — apply the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone
  there (it is not a core-`config.py` var).
- `tests/unit/test_config_tracker_dir.py` — **replace** `test_removed_legacy_alias_ignored`
  (which sets `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` and asserts `tracker_dir_override()` returns `None`
  — silent-ignore) with a new `test_removed_legacy_alias_errors` that asserts a
  `RemovedInputError` is raised instead. Also grep the test suite for any other test
  asserting silent-ignore of a now-tombstoned input and update it (the deliberate
  behavior change: silent-ignore → fail-loud).
- New CLI subcommand (`config validate` / `doctor`) + wiring (`show_config.py`) — the
  one place that **catches and formats `RemovedInputError`** into a report by inspecting
  the registry against env/files directly, rather than letting it abort via the raising
  `load_config()` path.
- MCP server entry path — the `except Exception` blocks at `mcp_server.py:302-312`/`:311`
  re-raise `RemovedInputError` explicitly; ensure a `RemovedInputError` raised during config load fails
  the write request / startup hard rather than being caught by the request-loop's
  top-level handler; audit that path for broad `except` handlers.
- `tests/golden/config_surface.json` (committed) + a pytest that imports the live
  allowed-key set (`_config_schema`) + env vars and the `_deprecations.py` registries,
  diffs against the golden file, and fails if a key is in neither the live set nor a
  tombstone/alias (anti-regression guard).
- `tests/unit/` — table-driven test over every seeded removed input: each retired
  input yields nonzero exit + old name + replacement + no store mutation; a
  genuinely-unknown key keeps the existing policy.
- `docs/config.md` — document the tombstone behaviour and the validate/doctor command.
- Out of scope: removed CLI flags already rejected by argument parsing and removed
  Python attributes that already raise — those are already fail-loud.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A `RemovedInput` tombstone registry exists and is applied at config discovery
  and CLI startup before defaults are chosen, at the top of `load_config()` before
  the `_RESULT_CACHE` lookup (so it fires on a warm cache hit) — verified by a test
  that sets a retired env var, primes the cache with a prior `load_config()`, and
  confirms the tombstone still fires.
- [ ] Setting a store/security/policy/sync-affecting retired input (e.g.
  `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`,
  `verify.require_verdict_for_close`) fails with a nonzero exit and a message naming
  the old name, the replacement, and the removed-in release, and does not mutate the
  store or reach the network — verified by a table-driven test that, for each seeded
  removed input, sets only that input, invokes config load / the smallest CLI command
  that initialises config, and asserts nonzero exit + old-name + replacement in the
  message + no store write (store files byte-unchanged) + no network call (enforced via
  `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive
  asserting `call_count == 0`).
- [ ] Operationally-inert retired inputs (`REBAR_PUSH`,
  `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`, `reconciler.lock_backend`, …) warn rather than
  error — the warning is emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path
  (`logging.warning` on the `rebar` logger, the same channel `warn_deprecated` uses),
  naming old→replacement→removed-in. Verified by a second sub-table in the table-driven
  test that captures the logger (via `caplog`) and asserts exit code 0, the log record
  contains the old name + replacement, and the process continues to normal completion.
- [ ] The error-class tombstone raises a `RemovedInputError` (a `BaseException`
  subclass, not `ConfigError`/`Exception`) — verified by the **rewritten**
  `test_config_tracker_dir.py` test (formerly `test_removed_legacy_alias_ignored`,
  which asserted silent-ignore): setting `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` and calling
  `tracker_dir()` now raises rather than falling back to `.tickets-tracker` (its
  `except ConfigError` cannot swallow a `BaseException`).
- [ ] Setting `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` and calling `LLMConfig.from_env()` surfaces the
  tombstone (naming `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS`) rather than silently ignoring it — verified
  by a dedicated test in the `rebar.llm` layer.
- [ ] The tombstone fails hard on the **MCP-server** path too (not only the CLI) —
  verified by an **in-process** test that primes the config cache, sets a retired
  error-class input, then calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g.
  `create_ticket`) and asserts `RemovedInputError` propagates out (not caught by the
  boot `except Exception` at `mcp_server.py:302-312`/`:311`, which are confirmed to
  re-raise it); a grep confirms no broad handler on the config→CLI→MCP path re-swallows
  it.
- [ ] A CI/anti-regression check fails if a recognised config key / env var is removed
  without a corresponding tombstone entry — implemented as a pytest test that imports
  the live allowed-key set (`_config_schema`'s allowed keys) + env vars and the
  tombstone/alias registries (`_deprecations.py`), diffs against a committed
  `tests/golden/config_surface.json`, and fails with a diff if any key appears in
  neither the live set nor a tombstone/alias.
- [ ] A genuinely-unknown env var / TOML key keeps the existing forward-compat
  policy (WARN by default; error only under `REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error`).
- [ ] `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` is the **one place that catches and
  formats `RemovedInputError`** into a report (rather than aborting): it inspects the
  registry against env + TOML + a leftover flat `.rebar/config.conf` directly, so it
  prints every tombstone/alias with old-name + replacement + removed-in and exits
  non-zero if any error-class input is present — verified by a test asserting
  `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR=old rebar config validate` exits non-zero with that report while
  a clean environment exits 0.
- [ ] `docs/config.md` documents the tombstone behaviour and the new command.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass, and the change lands via the
  Gerrit two-vote gate.
